blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Progressive. That was the word he used. It progresses. It doesn't stay the same — it moves, slowly, in one direction. And there's medication that slows it. Significantly, they said. It helps. I take it every morning and it helps.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: But there's no reversal. There's no fixing the architecture. The condition will keep doing what it does, just — more slowly, with the medication. I live in that in-between. The slowed-down version of something that's still moving.
The gondola pauses — another car loading below. The suspension of it, the held breath of the wheel.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I remember my mom asked if this was serious. The doctor said — 'it requires monitoring and lifelong management.' Which is the medical way of saying: yes. This is serious. We're just not going to say it like that.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I was sixteen. I remember looking at the beige wall and thinking: I am sixteen years old and I have just been told that my heart is tired.
A quiet. The wheel begins to move again.
Ethan has not moved. Has not interrupted. His hands are loose in his lap. He is completely, entirely here.
